scenario_config.name: lesson_bell_high
_dataset_notes: 15 teachers × 4 classrooms × 25 students = 1,500 students total (matches national_high).
Dataset is sized so ingest_targets_hot_week has ample variety at the higher peak rate; the load test does not exercise analytics.
Hot data: 1,500 students × 2 hot weeks × 3 runs × ~20 turns ≈ 180,000 hot turn events (T=20 per docs/research/ingest-capacity-model.md).
High-adoption variant of the lesson-bell burst regime; the medium-adoption variant lives in lesson_bell_medium.json.
_traffic_notes: Models the lesson-bell burst regime from docs/research/ingest-capacity-model.md §5 at the high-adoption peak (~75% of grade 4-6 pupils nationally).
Target peak: ~44 ingest/s, the high-adoption burst rate (60s drain window).
Stage profile: 10s ramp 0→44 + 60s hold at 44 + 30s drain 44→0 = 100s total.
VUs pre=90 ≈ 2× peak × 200ms p95 latency, with maxVUs=220 absorbing any worker-stall queueing. Scaled proportionally from lesson_bell_medium (peak 29, pre 60, max 150).
resource_sample_interval_seconds dropped to 2s so docker stats captures sub-minute dynamics of the burst.
Other traffic classes are zero — this scenario isolates the burst path on the ingest endpoint.
Pass condition (thesis context): no 5xx during the hold, post-burst Redis ingest_buffer depth drains within ~10s of drain end.
Expected outcome: tests whether the Redis-buffered ingest still absorbs the burst at 1.5× the medium-variant peak rate without 5xx, on the same 2 vCPU hardware.

load_health.note: Server handled this load without drops or failures. Latency numbers reflect the true cost of each request.
